caption.text:
A 6-ton iron pillar has stood in Delhi for over 1,600 years — and it has not rusted. Not a single significant layer of corrosion.

The Delhi Iron Pillar was forged during the Gupta period, likely under Chandragupta II, and later moved to its current location near the Qutub Minar. It is 99.72% pure wrought iron, over 7 meters tall, and has survived monsoons, pollution, and centuries of exposure.

In 2000, IIT Kanpur metallurgist R. Balasubramaniam published a peer-reviewed study in Corrosion Science identifying the secret: a crystalline iron hydrogen phosphate hydrate layer at the metal-scale interface, catalyzed by the pillar's high phosphorus content and Delhi's alternate wetting-drying cycles. The slag particles in the iron actually accelerated initial corrosion — which then enriched phosphorus at the surface, forming a self-healing protective film.

But here's the gap: modern metallurgists still cannot replicate this process at scale. The exact furnace temperatures, the precise carburization timing, and the surface-finishing hammering operation remain undocumented. We know what the pillar IS. We still don't fully know how they MADE it.

Would you trust a bridge built today to last 1,600 years?

text.line_2: in the 4th century CE. 99.72% pure iron.

text.line_1: IIT Kanpur found the secret: a crystalline
text.line_2: phosphate film. But we still cannot replicate it.
